15. How to Train Maintain a Great Sales Team.mp4 Alright, this is a three-part video. How to train a new hire, how to train your team on an ongoing basis, slash how to keep your salespeople great. So, first of all, I used to play sports when I was a kid, and my teams in high school, they sucked shit. It was really our coaches, in my opinion, that made us. It perpetuated the fact we sucked shit. They didn't do a good job bringing the talent out of us. Okay, now here's why. Basketball, football, baseball, all these different sports, they all had a commonality amongst my high school. None of them made us watch game footage. None of them really trained us on how to consistently run certain plays again and again and again and again. Sure, we'd practice. Sure, we'd do some plays, but we wouldn't do them as often as we needed to to be great at them. We would just do them occasionally. A lot of the time, because we'd lose games, instead of our coaches coming back during practice and making us focus on whatever we were weak at or kind of sucked at, all they would do is just discipline us with negative consequences. They'd make us run a lot; they'd make us do sprints. They'd make us just work out a lot more instead of actually putting us onto the field, putting us onto the court, et cetera, and making us better at, once again, whatever we needed strengthened. There's a few things in that that relate to our sales organizations. First of all, we have to have an ongoing training. We cannot just expect that there's a time where salespeople are all of a sudden trained. That's not how it works. Salespeople need an ongoing training regimen. Just as a professional athlete doesn't get to the NBA and all of a sudden just stop training, that's not how it works. If anything, they train harder. They do more activity in order to strengthen themselves against a greater level of competition that they now face. At no point has any great player you've ever known just stopped training. First of all, when we look at new hires, let's compare it to a restaurant. How many of you have gone to a restaurant before? When you sit down at your table, there are two waiters or waitresses that approach you instead of one. That's generally when there's a new hire that's being trained. There are two scenarios: either the waiter, the senior waiter who's been there a while, or the waitress is the one that's leading and the new person is just kind of idling by, watching and observing how that senior person behaves. That's how it needs to be in sales as well. We need our new hires, upon being brought into the organization, they need to do the behind-the-scenes training on an ongoing basis. They need to watch all the past calls. In addition to that, they need to just essentially sit back and tag along with the senior closers or salespeople in the organization for a little bit, at least for a week or two, so they can see what goes on during the calls live. Now, back to the restaurant analogy Once the person who's the new hire starts to get comfortable in their new position, what they'll do is they'll typically start to lead, and the senior waiter or waitress will step back and just kind of listen and wait just in case they need to step in. They're going to let that new hire lead the table, get everything done like the senior waiter or waitress would normally do. The senior waiter or waitress is just going to, once again, they're going to sit back, kind of monitor the situation, and they're going to step in when necessary, if necessary. They're going to monitor and make sure everything's fine. Same thing, that typically happens for another one to two weeks before that person eventually is on their own and doing their own thing. It is unbelievable to me in most sales organizations how when a new hire comes into the business, they have barely any training that they do. They do like a handful of calls with the business owner, and then they just start popping on calls. Get the person to just follow along, to tag along with somebody who's already in the sales process that's doing well in your team. Let them learn for one to two weeks. Give them a base salary or some compensation so they're not broke as shit just on commission only, at least for the first 30 to 60 days, as an example, with a very small commission for any deals that they close. That way, they have a livable wage. They can actually go through a training process where they can come out the other side good. Once they're actually fully ready to go, get them on a few calls with somebody who's in the sales organization to sit there and kind of listen in or at least watch the call recordings after they're done. See where there was some potential need for improvement or, like I said, if you have a senior member that can tag along and let that salesperson lead the call, let the senior person get the small commission even though that other person closed the deal. Like I said, if they're on a base salary, they deserve a small commission in the meantime. It's a great training process specifically for new hires to come into the business. You can also have some of the new hires use the roles that I've talked about in some of these other sales training videos, such as the cleaner role, or you can have your closers start as setters, at which point they can eventually ascend to a closer. Now, ongoing training is a necessity. We have to train our teams on an ongoing basis. Back in the day when I worked for Grant Cardone, I was very close with the sales organization because I had to work with them on a day-to-day basis to teach them how all the marketing stuff was working and how to use their CRMs. Answer all these questions they had for them on technical stuff, and I got to observe how their organization worked from the inside out. Before these people even walked in the door, they had to watch six different Cardone University videos. When your people show up to the office, how many video trainings do they need to consume before they are allowed onto the field to play? Probably not a lot, even if your team's fully remote. Have them train before they actually go out there and start taking calls for the day. It matters a lot. If I'm playing a sport, I don't just show up in my uniform and the buzzer rings, and I just start playing. I stretch first. I need to go and just warm up a little bit first. Then I go and play the game. It's the same thing in day-to-day life of your sales organization. They need to train before they walk into the office or before they start taking calls remotely. After they've trained, most of the best organizations I've worked with, including Grants, they do a morning meeting. So at 9 a. m. sharp in Grants' organization, all the people in the organization were on a morning meeting. They would walk them through, not just a bunch of bullshit, okay? They would walk them through the statistics of the organization. They would be transparent about everything. They would show people who are doing really well. They'd have those people share lessons with the rest of the organization. They would call out people who are doing bad and they would ask, hey, why are you doing bad? Can anybody here help you do better? They would make all of that transparent and public. It wouldn't just be a bunch of bullshit once again where it's like, all right guys, here at the morning meeting, really excited for the day. Let's go out there and crush it. It wouldn't say stupid shit like that They'd say real actionable data that can move the organization and the people that are part of the organization to a better place before they go out there and do their jobs for the day. Now the sales team, specifically in Grant's office, what they would do next is they would role-play. They'd spend about 30 to 45 minutes pairing up with one another, and they would role-play. The sales management would walk around the people who are role-playing. They'd listen in, they'd sometimes chime in, and they would add lessons and value when needed. Now to be clear, that's what makes great sales organizations. Before they even actually take calls, notice they did three things that would make them better for the day. Watch six training videos before they started. Did a morning meeting that wasn't full of bullshit and then role-played. Then they'd actually go out and get on sales calls and do the sales activity. Your salespeople want this in the organization. People who want to make the most money working in a sales role will want to be pushed and held accountable to the highest levels of their performance. Like I said, compared to any professional sports organization that you've ever watched, there is not a single chance in hell that once these people reach that level, they just stop. They don't, oh, I'm never gonna train again now Oh, I never need to practice again. I'm officially in the NBA or the NFL. That's the opposite of how it works, and you know it. Operate at a higher level. Stop being fucking lazy with your sales organization and your sales teams and get them to the point, once again, where you elevate the organization. In doing so, we make our salespeople great. But greatness is not something people step into every day with; greatness is something that is developed consistently. Greatness is something that we bring out of the people within our organizations Through holding them to higher standards, providing ongoing, consistent training, and making sure that we track everybody statistically, that we step in in real time when needed. If I ran a sports organization and I'm the coach, I'm not just sitting quiet on the bench while the game's being played. I'm watching the game. I'm yelling from the sidelines, telling my players what to do. There are multiple points throughout a game where there are timeouts called, where everybody regroups. There are multiple points throughout a game, depending on what sport it is, where there are quarters. Everybody regroups and reflects on what's happening while the game's being played. At halftime, at the end of the game, everybody just doesn't leave or sit around. You know, they go recap on what just happened during the game. This needs to happen in your organization as well. Throughout the day, you can't just let your salespeople run loose. You can't just let them just go and do whatever the fuck they want to do. You have to be involved with them in real time. Salespeople are the most important role within your organization. It is what makes the most money. You cannot let them just go out there on their own and just expect them to perform their greatest. You have to be available for them in real time to answer questions, to make sure that you're providing guidance when necessary. You can't just let them go and do their own thing. You will not bring out greatness within your team and make millions of dollars a month if you just act as a mediocre, average small business would. You have to act with what great sales organizations would do. The biggest businesses that exist do everything we just talked about, and they do it day in and day out because they wanna make a fuckload of money So moral of the story is to train a new hire, to train your team on an ongoing basis, and to bring out greatness; it requires a great system that extrapolates that greatness from each individual within your company. Don't sleep on this
